sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 113, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 88 actions; a strong score needs about 81 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN still doesn't work from the left side. The orange is at rows 33-36, cols 19-22. Looking at the image, the orange is now at the leftmost part of the horizontal path. The vertical path going down is below the orange's current position, but DOWN doesn't work.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, I see the path structure:
- The horizontal path is at rows ~33-36
- Below it, there's a vertical path going down from the left side
- But the orange can't go DOWN from its current position
Maybe the path doesn't connect directly below the orange. Let me look at the image again.
Actually, looking at the image, the vertical path going down seems to start from the LEFT end of the horizontal path. The orange is at cols 19-22, which is the left end. But DOWN doesn't work.
Hmm, maybe the path goes UP from the left end instead of DOWN? Or maybe I need to look at the path structure more carefully using segmentation.
Let me check the black path nodes to understand the connectivity.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me understand the black path structure
seg = current_frame.segmentation
black_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'B']
print(f"Black nodes: {len(black_nodes)}")
for n in black_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check adjacency to orange
orange_node = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O'][0]
print(f"\nOrange: id={orange_node['id']}")
adj = seg['adjacency_list']
orange_adj = [pair for pair in adj if orange_node['id'] in pair]
print(f"Orange adjacent to: {orange_adj}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python

  rows 7-56, cols 5-57, pixels=412
  rows 51-63, cols 2-11, pixels=40
  rows 54-57, cols 5-8, pixels=12

Orange adjacent to: [[32, 34]]

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The orange is adjacent to node 32. Let me check what node 32 is. Also, the black path is one large connected component (412 pixels). The orange can only move along this path.
Let me check what node 32 is and understand the path structure better. The orange is at rows 33-36, cols 19-22. The path seems to be a single connected black region.
Since DOWN doesn't work from the current position, maybe the path only allows LEFT/RIGHT movement at this segment. Let me try to understand the full path by looking at the black node's boundary.
Actually, let me reconsider. The orange moves along the black path. The path has specific segments where movement is allowed. Maybe the path goes:
- From the top grid area, the path goes right, down, left (horizontal segment)
- From the left end of the horizontal segment, the path goes DOWN
But DOWN doesn't work. Maybe the path actually goes UP from the left end?
Let me try UP from the current position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Orange at rows 33-36, cols 19-22
# Try UP

result = action(['UP'])
print(f"UP: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Orange: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Continue UP
result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
print(f"3x UP: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Orange: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
